description:
## Snow Water Equivalent (SWE)

This product was developed within the Digital Twin of Alps (DTA), a demonstration platform built by a Sinergise-led consortium and funded by the European Space Agency under Regional Initiative 3 (EXPRO+), ESA Contract No. 4000139281/22/I-DT. Running for two years from September 2022, the DTA combined Earth observation, numerical modelling and artificial intelligence into a single decision-support platform for water and risk management in the Alps, and serves as a stepping stone towards future Digital Twins  focused on mountainous regions. The Snow products available here were produced by Waterjade S.r.l. and serve as a development basis for the newer products available in the GTIF platform. These products are included in the GTIF platform for demonstration purposes.

Snow Water Equivalent (SWE) provides information on the amount of water contained within the snowpack. It is expressed as the depth of liquid water that would theoretically result if all the snow in a snowpack were instantaneously melted. SWE is calculated using the snow depth and its density, expressed here in millimeters, and acts as a critical metric for estimating water availability. In the Alps, most of the winter and early-spring precipitation above about 1,000 m falls as snow and stays locked in the snowpack until the melt season, when it slowly returns to the rivers between April and September. The snowpack is, in effect, a natural reservoir, and SWE measures how much is in it. Knowing how much water is stored, and where, helps with planning hydropower production, which supplies a large share of Alpine electricity, managing reservoirs, anticipating water availability for agriculture and downstream communities, and judging early on whether a season is heading towards drought or water abundance.

The maps were produced daily through the winter season (roughly October to June) at a resolution of 250 metres, covering the whole Alpine arc, an area of around 300,000 square kilometres. They were generated by the SnowMaps processing chain, built on the GEOtop physical model, which combines reanalysis weather data, local weather-station and snow-gauge measurements, and snow-cover information derived from Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 satellites. The full-Alps record covers the 2017 to 2022 winters (here a demonstration is provided over Austria).

This product is particularly useful for anyone whose activity depends on seasonal water availability, including hydropower operators and municipalities planning ahead for the season, as well as research centres feeding the data into their own models.
